message.content:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office (quote + claim premium/payout engine)
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: See the full kata specification in prompt.md at the run root (/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_10-51-43_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/prompt.md). Read that file in full — it contains all rules, examples, integration examples, and the CLI JSON input/output schema.

Key behaviors to cover (derive the test list from the spec's examples, simple → complex):
- Item base premiums (sword/amulet/staff/potion, components at 25 G each)
- Building block of exactly 3 alike components (60 G)
- "Alike" = same type (separate blocks per type)
- Premium modifiers: cursed +50%, high enchantment (>=5) +30%, loyalty (>=2 yrs) -20%, first insurance +10%, follow-up contract -15%, +5 G processing fee
- Modifier scope: item-specific modifiers on item base premium; policy-wide on policy total; fee last
- Rounding in MHPCO favor (premium up, payout down; intermediate kept as fractions)
- Claim processing: 100 G deductible per damage event, cap = 2x insurance sum, enchantment>=8 -> 50%, dragon material -> full, cap exhaustion across successive claims
- Edge cases: empty item list, unknown item type, claim referencing item not in policy, negative damage amount, more damages of a type than insured -> non-zero exit
- CLI reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout (results array)

Note there is a separate CLI entry point required at src/cli.ts, but the core logic should be in src/claim-office.ts and tested in src/claim-office.spec.ts.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the full specification.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

content.text: Now let me check the existing project structure.

input.command: ls -la src/ 2>/dev/null; ls -la example-mapping/ 2>/dev/null; cat package.json 2>/dev/null

content.text:
The `src/` directory is empty. Now let me work through the spec carefully to build the example mapping and test list.

Let me reason about a few computed values from the spec to make sure my test list has correct expected numbers.

**Rounding direction note**: premiums round up (ceil), payouts round down (floor), intermediate kept as fractions.

Let me verify the cap-exhaustion example: sword insured (sum 1000, cap 2000), two claims of 1500 each, single damage entry. First claim 1500 → full reimbursement, deductible 100 → 1400, cap remaining 600. Second claim desired 1400 (1500-100) reduced to remaining cap 600 → payout 600, remaining 0. Good.

Let me write the example-mapping file and the test list.

input.content:
# Example Mapping: MHPCO Claim Office

Feature file: `src/claim-office.ts` (core logic) + `src/cli.ts` (CLI entry).
Tests: `src/claim-office.spec.ts`.

Two operations: **quote** (compute premium for a list of items) and **claim**
(process a damage report against a policy created by an earlier quote).

---

## Rules

### R1 — Item base premiums and insurance values
Price list per main item:
- Sword: insurance value 1000 G, base premium 100 G
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components (rune, moonstone, ...): insurance value 250 G each, base premium
25 G per component.

### R2 — Building block of 3 alike components
A block of exactly 3 alike components costs 60 G base premium (instead of
3×25 = 75). Block requires **exactly 3**; surplus components are billed at
25 G each. Greedily form as many 3-blocks as possible per type.

### R3 — "Alike" = same type
A block is formed only from components of the **same type**. Different types
(rune vs moonstone) form separate blocks; they do not combine.

### R4 — Premium modifiers
- Cursed item: +50 % surcharge (item-specific, on the item's base premium)
- High enchantment (enchantment level ≥ 5): +30 % surcharge (item-specific)
- Loyalty (≥ 2 years with MHPCO): −20 % discount (policy-wide)
- First insurance: +10 % surcharge (policy-wide — see R8)
- Follow-up contract (each contract after the first): −15 % discount (policy-wide)
- Processing fee: +5 G, added at the very end (after everything)

### R5 — Modifier scope
- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the **affected
  item's base premium** only.
- Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up contract) apply
  to the **policy base premium** = sum of all item base premiums.
- The processing fee is added **last**.

Worked policy total order:
`policyTotal = sum(itemBase + itemSpecificSurcharges)`
then apply policy-wide percentage modifiers, then `+ 5 G fee`.

### R6 — Rounding in MHPCO's favor
- Premium → rounded **up** to whole G (ceil).
- Payout → rounded **down** to whole G (floor).
- Intermediate amounts are kept as exact fractions; only the final premium /
  payout is rounded.

### R7 — Claim processing
- 100 G deductible per damage **event** (= per damaged item / per entry in
  `damages`).
- Total payout per policy capped at **2 × insurance sum** (based on unmodified
  insurance values; premium modifiers do not raise the cap).
- High enchantment (≥ 8) on the damaged item → reimbursed at 50 % of the
  damage amount (the 50 % applies **before** the deductible).
- Dragon material → fully reimbursed.
- When both the ≥ 8 clause and dragon clause apply, the 50 % rule wins.
- Deductible applies after the percentage clause.
- Cap is shared and exhausted across successive claims on the same policy;
  a payout is reduced to the remaining cap.

### R8 — First insurance semantics (clarified ❓)
"First insurance" surcharge applies to **every item in a quote**, regardless
of customer history. It is NOT tied to customer's first-ever contract nor to
whether we've seen the item before. (Modeled as a policy-wide +10 % here,
consistent with the integration example arithmetic.)

### R9 — CLI
- Reads a JSON scenario from stdin: `{ customer: { yearsWithMHPCO }, steps: [...] }`.
- Steps processed sequentially; `claim` steps reference an earlier quote step
  by zero-based index in `policy`.
- Writes `{ results: [...] }` to stdout, same length/order as `steps`.
  - quote result: `{ premium: <int> }`
  - claim result: `{ payout: <int>, remainingCap: <int> }`
- Follow-up contract discount: a quote step is "first" if it's the customer's
  first quote in the scenario; later quotes get the −15 %.

### R10 — Error / edge conditions (CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr)
- Unknown item type in a quote (e.g. `broomstick`).
- Claim references an item not in the policy (wrong type, or unknown type).
- Damage entry with negative `amount`.
- `damages` contains more entries of a type than the policy covers
  (e.g. two sword damages, one sword insured) → whole claim rejected.
- Empty item list is NOT an error → premium 5 G (fee only).

---

## Examples (with expected values)

### Base premiums / blocks (R1, R2, R3)
- 2 runes → 50 G base (2×25)
- 3 runes → 60 G base (block)
- 4 runes → 100 G base (block of 3 + 1 single = 60 + 25)... NOTE spec says
  "4 runes → 100 G (no block — block requires exactly 3)". So 4 runes = 100 G,
  i.e. 4×25, NOT 60+25. Block applies only when count is exactly a multiple
  of 3? Re-read: "7 runes → 175 G". 7 = 175. 175 = 7×25. And 3→60, 6→? Spec
  only gives 3→60. For 7: if greedy blocks, 2 blocks (6) + 1 = 120+25 = 145,
  but spec says 175 = 7×25. So the block applies ONLY when the count is
  EXACTLY 3. Any other count → all components billed singly at 25 G each.
  - 2 runes → 50, 3 runes → 60, 4 runes → 100 (4×25), 7 runes → 175 (7×25).
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block; 3×25, different types) (R3)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks, 60+60) (R3)

### Modifier scope (R5)
- Policy: cursed sword (base 100) + plain amulet (base 60) → policy base 160;
  cursed surcharge = 50 (50 % of sword's 100, not of 160) → 210 before
  policy-wide modifiers and fee.

### Modifier thresholds (R4)
- Exactly 2 years → loyalty applies.
- Sword enchantment exactly 5 → high-enchantment applies.
- Sword enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment.
- Dragon sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 → payout 400
  (≥8 clause: 50 % → 500; then deductible 100 → 400).

### Claim clauses (R7)
- Dragon attack: insured sword damaged 500 + insured amulet damaged 300 →
  payout 600 (deductible once per item: (500−100)+(300−100)=400+200=600).
- Regular sword (steel, ench 3), damage 500 → 400 (500−100).
- Rune (value 250), damage 200 → 100 (200−100; no clauses for components).
- Dragon sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → 400 (50 % wins: 500, −100).
- Dragon sword, ench 5, damage 800 → 700 (dragon full: 800, −100).
- Steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → 400 (50 %: 500, −100).

### Multiple same-type items (R7, R10)
- Two swords → insurance sum 2000, cap 4000.
- Dragon attack damages both swords (two sword entries) → each its own
  deductible.
- More sword damages than swords insured → CLI non-zero exit, claim rejected.

### Cap (R7)
- Sword + amulet → insurance sum 1600, cap 3200.
- Cursed sword (value 1000, premium 165) → cap 2000 (unmodified value).
- Sword + 3 runes (block) → insurance sum 1750 (1000 + 3×250); block
  discount affects premium only, not sum.
- Sword (sum 1000, cap 2000), two successive claims of 1500 each:
  - claim 1 → payout 1400 (1500−100), remaining cap 600.
  - claim 2 → desired 1400 reduced to remaining 600 → payout 600, remaining 0.

### Rounding (R6)
- Premium 197.5 → 198 (up).
- Payout 350.5 → 350 (down).

### Edge cases (R10)
- Empty item list → premium 5 (fee only).
- Unknown item type in quote → non-zero exit, stderr, no results on stdout.
- Claim references item not in policy → non-zero exit.
- Damage amount −200 → non-zero exit.

### Integration (R4, R5, R8)
- Newcomer (0 yrs, no previous contract), cursed sword (steel, ench 3) →
  premium 165. (100 base + 50 curse + 10 first insurance = 160; + 5 fee = 165.)
  Arithmetic: itemSpecific → 100 + 50 = 150 (curse). policy-wide: first
  insurance +10 % of policy base 100 = +10 → 160; fee +5 → 165.
  NOTE: the +10 first-insurance is 10 % of the policy base premium (100),
  giving 10, applied to the surcharged total 150 → 160. Confirmed by numbers.
- Long-standing customer's 2nd contract (3 yrs), cursed sword (steel, ench 7)
  → premium 160.
  (100 base + 50 curse + 30 high ench − 20 loyalty + 10 first ins − 15
  follow-up = 155; + 5 fee = 160.)
  Each %-modifier computed on the policy base premium 100:
  curse 50 (on item base 100), high ench 30 (on item base 100), loyalty
  −20 (20 % of 100), first insurance +10 (10 % of 100), follow-up −15 (15 %
  of 100). Item base 100 + 50 + 30 − 20 + 10 − 15 = 155; + 5 = 160.
  => All percentage modifiers are computed against the relevant base (item
  base for item-specific, policy base premium for policy-wide), then summed
  additively, not compounded. This matches both integration examples.

---

## Interface contract (verbatim field names — binding)

**Input (stdin):**
```json
{
  "customer": { "yearsWithMHPCO": 5 },
  "steps": [
    { "op": "quote",
      "items": [ { "type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false } ] },
    { "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": { "cause": "fire", "damages": [ { "itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200 } ] } }
  ]
}
```

**Output (stdout):**
```json
{ "results": [ { "premium": 65 }, { "payout": 100, "remainingCap": 1100 } ] }
```
(amulet premium for 5-yr customer, ench 2, not cursed: base 60; loyalty −20 %
= −12, first insurance +10 % = +6, → 60 − 12 + 6 = 54; + 5 fee = 59. payout
200 − 100 = 100; cap = 2×600 = 1200, remaining 1100. These are illustrative;
the spec only fixes the shape with `<integer>` placeholders, so the CLI shape
test asserts structure/keys, not these exact derived numbers.)

Item object fields: `type`, `material?`, `enchantment?`, `cursed?`.
Damage object fields: `itemType`, `amount`.
Claim step fields: `op`, `policy`, `incident{ cause, damages[] }`.

---

## Questions / Clarifications (❓ from spec — resolved)
- Q: "alike" = same type or same family? → **Same type** (R3). Separate blocks
  per type.
- Q: cursed surcharge whole policy or just cursed item? → **Just the cursed
  item's base premium** (R5).
- Q: two of the same item? → Each is a separate insured item; insurance sum
  adds up; each damage entry has its own deductible (R7).
- Q: "first insurance" = first ever contract or first time seeing item? →
  **Applies to every item in a quote regardless of history** (R8).

---

## Per-test rationale (each it.todo below)
1. amulet base 60 — R1 simplest single item.
2. sword base 100 — R1.
3. staff base 80 — R1.
4. potion base 40 — R1.
5. single rune base 25 — R1 component.
6. empty item list → 5 (fee only) — R10/R6 edge, simplest fee.
7. 2 runes → 50 base — R2 below-block.
8. 3 runes → 60 base (block) — R2.
9. 4 runes → 100 base (no block) — R2 exactly-3 rule.
10. 7 runes → 175 base — R2.
11. 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 (no block, diff types) — R3.
12. 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 (two blocks) — R3.
13. cursed sword item-specific surcharge: base 100 +50 = 150 (+5 fee, newcomer
    first-ins) integration → 165 — R4/R5 cursed + integration newcomer.
14. high-enchantment threshold ench 5 applies — R4.
15. enchantment 4 no surcharge — R4.
16. loyalty exactly 2 years applies — R4.
17. multi-item modifier scope: cursed sword + plain amulet → 210 before
    policy-wide & fee — R5.
18. integration newcomer cursed sword → premium 165 — R4/R5/R8.
19. integration long-standing 2nd contract cursed ench7 sword → 160 —
    R4/R5/R8/R9 follow-up.
20. premium rounding 197.5 → 198 (up) — R6.
21. claim: regular sword damage 500 → payout 400 — R7 standard.
22. claim: rune damage 200 → payout 100 — R7 component no clause.
23. claim: high-ench (≥8) sword 50 % then deductible (dragon ench8 dmg1000 →
    400) — R7.
24. claim: dragon material full reimbursement (dragon ench5 dmg800 → 700) — R7.
25. claim: steel ench9 dmg1000 → 400 (50 % clause) — R7.
26. claim: dragon ench9 dmg1000 → 400 (50 % wins over dragon) — R7.
27. claim: deductible per damaged item (sword500 + amulet300 → 600) — R7.
28. cap = 2× insurance sum (sword+amulet sum1600 cap3200) — R7.
29. cap from unmodified value (cursed sword cap 2000) — R7.
30. insurance sum with block (sword + 3 runes → 1750) — R7.
31. payout rounding 350.5 → 350 (down) — R6.
32. cap exhaustion across two claims (1400 then 600, remaining 0) — R7.
33. two swords insurance sum 2000 cap 4000 — R7.
34. two swords both damaged, each own deductible — R7.
35. CLI: reads stdin JSON, writes results array with premium key — R9 shape.
36. CLI: claim step result has payout + remainingCap keys — R9 shape.
37. CLI error: unknown item type → non-zero exit, no stdout results — R10.
38. CLI error: claim references item not in policy → non-zero exit — R10.
39. CLI error: negative damage amount → non-zero exit — R10.
40. CLI error: more damages of a type than insured → non-zero exit — R10.
